sectors.name: Federal Government
sectors.description: The President, six cabinet departments, and their documented financial/influence ties to private individuals.

people.name: Donald Trump
people.title: President of the United States
people.bio: 47th President of the United States, inaugurated January 20, 2025. Appoints and directs the heads of all executive departments.

people.name: Pete Hegseth
people.title: Secretary of Defense (also uses "Secretary of War" per Executive Order 14347)
people.bio: Former Fox News host and Army National Guard veteran who deployed to Guantanamo Bay, Iraq, and Afghanistan. Led veterans' advocacy groups Concerned Veterans for America and Vets for Freedom before joining the cabinet. Confirmed January 24, 2025 by a 51-50 Senate vote.

people.name: Steve Feinberg
people.title: Deputy Secretary of Defense
people.bio: Co-founder and former CEO of Cerberus Capital Management, a private-equity firm with extensive defense-industry holdings including past ownership of DynCorp International. Now serves as the Pentagon's chief operating officer. Confirmed March 14, 2025.

people.name: Marc Andreessen
people.title: Co-founder, Andreessen Horowitz (a16z)
people.bio: Silicon Valley venture capitalist and co-creator of the Mosaic/Netscape web browser, now a leading investor in AI, defense-tech, and crypto startups. Appointed to the reconstituted Defense Policy Board, an official DoD advisory committee, in June 2026. FEC filings show a $2.5 million personal contribution to a pro-Trump Super PAC in October 2024; combined giving with his firm exceeds $24 million for the 2024 cycle per OpenSecrets.
people.source: Bloomberg, Jun 2026; OpenSecrets donor records; Military.com

people.name: Scott Bessent
people.title: Secretary of the Treasury
people.bio: Former hedge fund manager who led macro investing at Soros Fund Management before founding his own fund, Key Square Group. Confirmed January 27, 2025 by a 68-29 Senate vote, the 79th Treasury Secretary.

people.name: Francis Brooke
people.title: Nominee for Deputy Secretary (pending confirmation as of July 2026); Acting Under Secretary for International Affairs
people.bio: Nominated May 11, 2026 to be Deputy Secretary after predecessor Michael Faulkender departed in August 2025. The Senate Finance Committee held his confirmation hearing on July 16, 2026, with no floor vote yet held.

people.name: Jason Granet
people.title: Chief Investment Officer, BNY (Bank of New York Mellon)
people.bio: Joined BNY in 2021 after more than 20 years at Goldman Sachs, most recently heading International Liquidity Portfolio Management. Has served on the Treasury Borrowing Advisory Committee since November 2022 and is set to become its chair starting the August 2026 meeting.
people.source: Treasury.gov TBAC members list; Bloomberg Law

people.name: Marco Rubio
people.title: Secretary of State (also concurrently National Security Advisor since May 1, 2025)
people.bio: Served as U.S. Senator from Florida from 2011-2025 and earlier as Speaker of the Florida House of Representatives. First Latino American to serve as Secretary of State. Confirmed January 20, 2025 by a unanimous 99-0 Senate vote.

people.name: Christopher Landau
people.title: Deputy Secretary of State
people.bio: Clerked for Justice Antonin Scalia and then-Judge Clarence Thomas, then spent 25 years at Kirkland & Ellis chairing its appellate practice before joining Quinn Emanuel. Previously served as U.S. Ambassador to Mexico (2019-2021). Confirmed March 24, 2025.

people.name: Todd Blanche
people.title: Acting Attorney General (nominated for permanent confirmation; hearing held July 15-16, 2026, outcome pending)
people.bio: Assistant U.S. Attorney in the SDNY's violent-crimes unit (2006-2014), later a white-collar defense partner at WilmerHale and Cadwalader who defended Paul Manafort. Became Trump's personal defense attorney in 2023, then confirmed Deputy Attorney General in March 2025 before becoming Acting AG on April 2, 2026.

people.name: Pam Bondi
people.title: Attorney General, January 2025 - April 2026 (removed from office)
people.bio: Served as the 87th U.S. Attorney General before being removed by President Trump on April 2, 2026, reportedly amid frustration over her handling of the Epstein files. Her tenure included the clemency decision described below.

people.name: Elizabeth Fago
people.title: Healthcare entrepreneur, Buffalo, NY
people.bio: FEC records show a $1 million donation to Make America Great Again Inc., a pro-Trump Super PAC, on April 3, 2025. Her son Paul Walczak, convicted of federal tax crimes, received a full presidential pardon on April 23, 2025 - 20 days later - processed through DOJ's Office of the Pardon Attorney. Reporting indicates the clemency application cited her donation; no quid pro quo has been legally established.
people.source: Buffalo News; Rolling Stone; DOJ Clemency Grants records

people.name: Robert F. Kennedy Jr.
people.title: Secretary of Health and Human Services
people.bio: Practiced as an environmental attorney for decades with the Natural Resources Defense Council and Waterkeeper Alliance before becoming a prominent vaccine-safety advocate. Ran for president in 2024 as an independent before endorsing Trump. Confirmed February 13, 2025 by a 52-48 Senate vote.

people.name: Tony Robbins
people.title: Motivational speaker and author
people.bio: Appointed to HHS/CMS's 18-member Healthcare Advisory Committee (announced March 2026), which advises the Secretary and CMS Administrator on Medicare/Medicaid policy. Widely described in coverage as a Trump donor, though a specific FEC contribution amount could not be independently verified.
people.source: Bloomberg Law; STAT News, Mar 2026

people.name: Calley Means
people.title: Senior Advisor, HHS Office of the Secretary; co-founder, TrueMed Inc.
people.bio: Advised Kennedy on nutrition policy as a Special Government Employee starting March 2025, then moved to a full-time, paid Senior Advisor role in November 2025. HHS ethics filings showed he held $25-50 million in shares of TrueMed, a company he co-founded, while advising on related policy - prompting a formal congressional oversight inquiry.
people.source: Rep. Auchincloss oversight letter; IBTimes UK

people.name: Brooke Rollins
people.title: Secretary of Agriculture
people.bio: Founder, president and CEO of the America First Policy Institute (2021-2025) and earlier president/CEO of the Texas Public Policy Foundation. Served as Director of the White House Domestic Policy Council during Trump's first term. Confirmed February 13, 2025 by a 72-28 Senate vote.

people.name: Stephen Vaden
people.title: Deputy Secretary of Agriculture
people.bio: Yale Law School graduate who previously served as USDA General Counsel during Trump's first term, then as a judge on the U.S. Court of International Trade (2020-2025) before returning to USDA. Sworn in July 7, 2025.

people.name: Fabio Sandri
people.title: President and CEO, Pilgrim's Pride Corporation
people.bio: Leads the second-largest U.S. poultry processor, majority-owned by Brazil's JBS SA. Pilgrim's Pride donated $5 million to the Trump-Vance 2025 Presidential Inaugural Committee - the largest disclosed corporate donation to that committee - while the company remains directly regulated by USDA's Food Safety and Inspection Service. Sen. Elizabeth Warren sent a formal oversight letter in May 2025 questioning the donation's proximity to USDA decisions affecting the poultry industry.
people.source: OpenSecrets 2025 Inauguration Donors; Sen. Warren oversight letter, May 2025

people.name: Doug Burgum
people.title: Secretary of the Interior
people.bio: Mortgaged inherited North Dakota farmland in 1983 to help fund Great Plains Software, which he grew and sold to Microsoft for $1.1 billion in 2001. Served as the 33rd Governor of North Dakota (2016-2024) before joining the cabinet. Confirmed January 30, 2025 by a 79-18 Senate vote.

people.name: Katharine MacGregor
people.title: Deputy Secretary of the Interior
people.bio: Previously Vice President at NextEra Energy and held senior Capitol Hill staff roles; also held this same Deputy Secretary post during Trump's first term. Confirmed May 14, 2025.

people.name: Harold Hamm
people.title: Founder and Executive Chairman, Continental Resources
people.bio: Built one of the largest independent oil producers in the U.S. around North Dakota's Bakken Shale. A longtime personal donor to Burgum - backing both of his gubernatorial campaigns, giving $250,000 to Burgum's 'Best of America' PAC, and $50 million to Burgum's planned presidential library. Reporting based on internal emails shows Interior issued Continental Resources dozens of new Wyoming drilling permits in August 2025 after Burgum announced a 28-day permit-review cap at an event Hamm hosted that April.
people.source: High Country News; Revolving Door Project; Center for Western Priorities

people.name: Howard Lutnick
people.title: Secretary of Commerce
people.bio: Chairman and CEO of Cantor Fitzgerald from 1996 until stepping down in 2025 to join the cabinet, a 40-plus-year Wall Street bond-trading career. Confirmed February 18, 2025 by a 51-45 Senate vote.

people.name: Paul Dabbar
people.title: Deputy Secretary of Commerce
people.bio: Most recently President/CEO of Bohr Quantum Technology; previously Under Secretary for Science at the Department of Energy during Trump's first term. Confirmed June 25, 2025 by a 56-40 Senate vote.

people.name: Brandon Lutnick
people.title: Chairman, Cantor Fitzgerald
people.bio: Took over Cantor Fitzgerald when his father Howard divested to join the cabinet, controlling trusts (also benefiting brother Kyle) that hold the family's stake rather than a blind trust. In 2026 Cantor Fitzgerald acted as placement agent on a $1.6 billion financing deal for USA Rare Earth Inc., a critical-minerals company concurrently in an arrangement with the Commerce Department; four members of Congress sent formal oversight letters demanding records of Commerce-Cantor Fitzgerald contacts.
people.source: CNBC; Sen. Warren/Van Hollen/Wyden press releases, 2026

people.name: Keith Sonderling
people.title: Acting Secretary of Labor (nominated for permanent confirmation; Senate HELP Committee hearing held July 16, 2026, vote scheduled July 23, 2026)
people.bio: Confirmed Deputy Secretary of Labor in March 2025, then designated Acting Secretary on April 20, 2026 after Lori Chavez-DeRemer resigned amid a reported misconduct investigation. Previously an EEOC Commissioner (2020-2024) and led DOL's Wage and Hour Division under the first Trump administration.

people.name: Marc Freedman
people.title: Vice President, Workplace Policy, U.S. Chamber of Commerce
people.bio: Has led the Chamber's workplace-policy advocacy since 2004. A registered federal lobbyist and the Chamber's named spokesperson on DOL's February 2026 proposed rule redefining independent-contractor status, which the Chamber-led business coalition has said it is prepared to challenge in court.
people.source: U.S. Chamber of Commerce; DOL rulemaking notice, Feb 2026

people.name: Scott Turner
people.title: Secretary of Housing and Urban Development
people.bio: Former NFL cornerback (1995-2003) who later served two terms as a Texas state representative and led the White House Opportunity and Revitalization Council in Trump's first term. Confirmed February 5, 2025 by a 55-44 Senate vote.

people.name: Andrew Hughes
people.title: Deputy Secretary of Housing and Urban Development
people.bio: Served as HUD Chief of Staff under Secretary Ben Carson (2018-2021) and again under Turner in 2025 before his confirmation on June 10, 2025. Reportedly the youngest Deputy Secretary in HUD history.

people.name: Robert Broeksmit
people.title: President and CEO, Mortgage Bankers Association
people.bio: Leads the MBA's advocacy on federal mortgage policy. MBA's Senate LDA disclosures name HUD as a lobbied agency, and Broeksmit met directly with Secretary Turner in 2025 before sending HUD formal recommendations in 2026 on FHA multifamily mortgage insurance in Opportunity Zones.
people.source: MBA NewsLink; Scotsman Guide; Senate LDA filings

people.name: Sean Duffy
people.title: Secretary of Transportation
people.bio: Represented Wisconsin's 7th District in the U.S. House (2011-2019) and later co-hosted Fox Business's 'The Bottom Line.' Confirmed January 28, 2025 by a 77-22 Senate vote.

people.name: Steven Bradbury
people.title: Deputy Secretary of Transportation
people.bio: Previously DOT General Counsel (2017-2021) and briefly Acting Secretary of Transportation in January 2021. Confirmed March 11, 2025 by a 51-46 Senate vote.

people.name: Jeff Miller
people.title: Founder and CEO, Miller Strategies LLC
people.bio: A registered federal lobbyist who lobbied DOT in 2025 for at least nine clients, and served as Finance Chair of Trump's 2025 inaugural committee. ProPublica reporting found Miller and his firm's COO donated to the congressional campaign of Secretary Duffy's son-in-law, who is running for Duffy's former Wisconsin House seat.
people.source: ProPublica; Wisconsin Watch, 2026

people.name: Chris Wright
people.title: Secretary of Energy
people.bio: MIT-trained mechanical engineer who founded Pinnacle Technologies and later Liberty Energy, which grew into North America's second-largest hydraulic fracturing company under his leadership. Confirmed February 3, 2025 by a 59-38 Senate vote.

people.name: James Danly
people.title: Deputy Secretary of Energy
people.bio: Former FERC Commissioner and briefly FERC Chairman (2020-2021); was an Energy Regulatory Group partner at Skadden, Arps before returning to government. Confirmed May 13, 2025 by a 52-44 Senate vote.

people.name: Joe Craft
people.title: President and CEO, Alliance Resource Partners
people.bio: Leads the third-largest coal producer in the eastern U.S. Formally appointed by Secretary Wright to the Secretary of Energy Advisory Board on July 9, 2026 for a two-year term. A longtime major Republican donor who, with wife and former UN Ambassador Kelly Craft, co-chairs the RNC's Presidential Trust fundraising vehicle.
people.source: DOE advisory board announcement, Jul 2026; Center for Public Integrity donor profile

people.name: Linda McMahon
people.title: Secretary of Education
people.bio: Co-founder and former CEO/president of WWE (1980-2009) and Administrator of the Small Business Administration in Trump's first term. Confirmed March 3, 2025 by a 51-45 Senate vote; has described her mandate as working 'to work herself out of a job' amid ongoing efforts to shift the department's functions elsewhere.

people.name: Nicholas Kent
people.title: Under Secretary of Education, delegated the duties of Deputy Secretary since February 2026
people.bio: Previously Deputy Secretary of Education for Virginia under Gov. Glenn Youngkin, and before that Chief Policy Officer at a for-profit college trade association. Confirmed Under Secretary on August 4, 2025; his predecessor nominee for Deputy Secretary withdrew amid Senate opposition.

people.name: Jeffrey Yass
people.title: Co-founder, Susquehanna International Group
people.bio: The nation's largest school-choice/voucher megadonor. FEC filings show over $82 million in federal-level spending ahead of the 2026 midterms, including millions to school-choice advocacy vehicles. He is a direct financial beneficiary of the Educational Choice for Children Act, a federal tax-credit scholarship program enacted July 2025 that the Department of Education is now implementing.
people.source: OpenSecrets donor records; ED.gov fact sheet; Forbes

people.name: Doug Collins
people.title: Secretary of Veterans Affairs
people.bio: Former U.S. Representative for Georgia's 9th District (2013-2021) and a longtime U.S. Air Force Reserve chaplain. Confirmed February 4, 2025 by a 77-23 Senate vote.

people.name: Paul Lawrence
people.title: Deputy Secretary of Veterans Affairs
people.bio: Directs department-wide policy and operations as VA's chief operating officer. Confirmed March 27, 2025.

people.name: Larry Ellison
people.title: Co-founder and Executive Chairman, Oracle Corporation
people.bio: Oracle's Cerner/Oracle Health division holds VA's Electronic Health Record modernization contract, estimated at roughly $37 billion in total value. Oracle's Senate LDA filings specifically list VA electronic-records oversight as a lobbied issue, and Ellison was appointed to the President's Council of Advisors on Science and Technology in March 2026.
people.source: Senate LDA filings; OpenSecrets; Revolving Door Project

people.name: Markwayne Mullin
people.title: Secretary of Homeland Security
people.bio: Former U.S. Senator (2023-2026) and Representative (2013-2023) from Oklahoma, an enrolled Cherokee Nation citizen and former professional MMA fighter. Confirmed March 23, 2026 by a 54-45 Senate vote, succeeding Kristi Noem after her removal that March.

people.name: Troy Edgar
people.title: Deputy Secretary of Homeland Security
people.bio: Former IBM executive who served as DHS's CFO during Trump's first term. Confirmed March 6, 2025 by a 53-43 Senate vote.

people.name: George Zoley
people.title: Founder, Chairman and CEO, The GEO Group
people.bio: GEO Group and its subsidiaries have been awarded more than $1 billion in federal contracts for ICE detention since Trump retook office. Zoley personally contributed to Trump's Save America fundraising committee and a joint GOP committee per FEC records; GEO Group's PAC was the first corporate PAC to max out to Trump's campaign, and the company donated $500,000 to the 2025 inaugural committee.
people.source: FEC records; CREW investigation; OpenSecrets

people.name: Alex Karp
people.title: CEO and co-founder, Palantir Technologies
people.bio: In February 2026, DHS signed a department-wide blanket purchase agreement with Palantir worth up to $1 billion, letting ICE, CBP, FEMA and CISA buy its software without competitive bidding. Karp personally contributed $1 million to Trump's inauguration fund in early 2025, a notable shift given his prior support for Democratic candidates.
people.source: The Hill; CNBC, 2026

people.name: Susie Wiles
people.title: White House Chief of Staff
people.bio: The first woman to serve as White House Chief of Staff, in office since January 20, 2025. Widely described in reporting as commanding unmatched authority in Trump's orbit, often characterized as the last person he consults before major decisions.

people.name: Stephen Miller
people.title: Deputy Chief of Staff for Policy and Homeland Security Advisor
people.bio: In office since January 20, 2025, simultaneously holding both the policy and homeland-security-advisor portfolios, giving him unusually broad reach across immigration and domestic policy. Previously a senior advisor in Trump's first term.

people.name: Karoline Leavitt
people.title: White House Press Secretary
people.bio: The youngest White House Press Secretary in history, in office since January 20, 2025. Previously communications director for Rep. Elise Stefanik and Trump's 2024 campaign press secretary.

people.name: Dan Scavino
people.title: Deputy Chief of Staff; Director, White House Presidential Personnel Office
people.bio: A Trump aide since 2015, serving as Deputy Chief of Staff since January 2025 and additionally directing the office that vets administration hires and fires since October 2025, combining physical proximity to Trump with control over personnel decisions.

people.name: Mark Levin
people.title: Syndicated radio host and Fox News host
people.bio: Former chief of staff to Reagan-era Attorney General Ed Meese. Appointed by Trump to DHS's Homeland Security Advisory Council in April 2025; has given Trump phone interviews for his radio show and was invited to address the White House Hanukkah Reception in December 2025, though he has also publicly broken with Trump on some policy, including the 2026 Iran deal.
people.source: Variety; Breitbart; Washington Times, 2025-2026

people.name: Laura Loomer
people.title: Political activist
people.bio: Met with Trump in the Oval Office on April 2, 2025, presenting a list of roughly a dozen National Security Council staffers she alleged were disloyal; at least four were fired the next day. Multiply sourced by CNN, Axios, and NPR; Trump publicly confirmed speaking with her and that she'd made personnel recommendations while downplaying her role.
people.source: CNN; Axios; NPR, Apr 2025

people.name: Jared Kushner
people.title: Founder, Affinity Partners; Trump's son-in-law
people.bio: Trump announced Kushner as 'Special Envoy for Peace' in February 2026, though the White House separately said the role 'would not be an official one.' Documented activity includes joining envoy Steve Witkoff on Ukraine peace talks in Moscow and Iran nuclear negotiations in Geneva; congressional letters have raised conflict-of-interest concerns over his Middle East business dealings.
people.source: The Hill; CREW; Times of Israel, 2026

people.name: Elon Musk
people.title: CEO, Tesla and SpaceX
people.bio: Led the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) before departing mid-2025, followed by a public feud with Trump that summer. The two have since visibly reconciled - sharing dinner at Mar-a-Lago and traveling together to China - with Trump calling him 'a friend of mine again' in June 2026, though reporting characterizes the relationship as a fragile, fluctuating truce rather than steady access.
people.source: Bloomberg; Fox News; Axios, 2026

major donor, son pardoned during her tenure

sectors.name: Finance
sectors.description: Bank executives, hedge fund managers, and regulators who move capital markets.

people.name: Richard Voss
people.title: CEO, Meridian Capital Group
people.bio: Runs one of the largest private equity firms in the country, with a reputation for aggressive but disciplined dealmaking.

people.name: Elena Marchetti
people.title: Chair, Federal Reserve Regional Bank
people.bio: Sets interest rate policy recommendations and is a frequent voice in national economic debates.

people.name: Marcus Webb
people.title: Founder, Northbridge Hedge Fund
people.bio: Built a multi-billion dollar fund from scratch; known for high-conviction macro bets and a small, secretive inner circle.

people.name: Priya Chandrasekaran
people.title: Managing Partner, Vantage Ventures
people.bio: Top venture investor who sits on the boards of several unicorn startups and bridges finance with tech.

sectors.name: Technology
sectors.description: Founders, investors, and executives steering the direction of the tech industry.

people.name: Adrian Cho
people.title: CEO, Nimbus AI
people.bio: Founded one of the leading frontier AI labs after a career at two major tech giants. Sets the pace for the industry's AI roadmap.

people.name: Sasha Kowalski
people.title: General Partner, Ridgeline Ventures
people.bio: Early backer of three unicorns, now one of the most sought-after board members in Silicon Valley.

people.name: Devon Okafor
people.title: CTO, Helix Systems
people.bio: Architect behind several widely adopted open-source infrastructure projects; quietly influential among engineering leadership.

people.name: Naomi Fischer
people.title: Founder & CEO, Fischer Robotics
people.bio: Serial entrepreneur pushing autonomous systems into manufacturing; frequently advises federal tech policy committees.

sectors.name: Media
sectors.description: Network executives, publishers, and platform owners shaping the national narrative.

sectors.name: Energy
sectors.description: Oil, gas, and renewable energy executives with outsized influence over policy and markets.

sectors.name: Defense
sectors.description: Contractors, Pentagon officials, and intelligence community leaders.
